verses.commentary: Greater revelation demands greater attention. To drift (pararreō) means to slip away like a ship loosing its mooring. The danger is not violent rejection but gradual neglect. Apathy kills as surely as apostasy.
The Law delivered through angels was binding; every transgression received just punishment. If angelic mediation carried such weight, how much more the message spoken by the Son Himself?

The logic is inescapable: if the Law could not be ignored without penalty, how shall we escape if we neglect salvation spoken by the Lord? 'Neglect' is the danger—not active rebellion, but passive indifference.
God testified to this salvation through signs, wonders, miracles, and gifts of the Holy Spirit distributed according to His will. The gospel came with divine authentication—visible, undeniable proof.
The world to come is not subjected to angels but to redeemed humanity. Angels served the old order; Christ inaugurates the new creation where humans reign with Him.

Psalm 8 is quoted: What is mankind that God is mindful of them? The question highlights humanity's smallness yet God's astonishing care. This psalm finds ultimate fulfillment in Christ, the true human.

Humanity was made lower than angels temporarily, then crowned with glory and honor. This describes both Adam's original design and Christ's incarnation. Jesus took the lower position to restore humanity's higher calling.
All things were subjected to humanity in God's design. Yet we do not see this fulfilled—creation is still broken, humanity still fallen. The promise awaits Christ's return.

But we do see Jesus. He was made lower than angels for a little while, then crowned with glory because He suffered death. His suffering secured our salvation. By grace He tasted death for everyone—substitutionary atonement.

It was fitting that God, for whom and through whom all things exist, should make the pioneer (archēgos) of salvation perfect through suffering. Christ's sufferings were not accidental but divinely appointed to accomplish redemption.

Christ (the sanctifier) and believers (the sanctified) share the same family. He is not ashamed to call us brothers and sisters. Astonishing condescension: the sinless Son claims kinship with sinners saved by grace.
Psalm 22:22 applied to Christ: He declares God's name to His brothers and praises God in the assembly. Jesus leads worship. He is both the offering and the worship leader.
Isaiah 8:17-18 quoted: Christ trusts the Father and stands with the children God gave Him. He identifies fully with His people, sharing their dependence on God.

Since the children share flesh and blood, He likewise partook of the same nature. Why? To destroy death and him who holds its power—the devil. The Incarnation was strategic: He became what we are to defeat our greatest enemies.
Christ freed those enslaved by fear of death. Death held humanity hostage; Christ broke the chains. Believers need no longer live under death's shadow.
He helps not angels but Abraham's descendants—humanity. His concern is for fallen humans, not fallen angels. The Incarnation was for us.
Christ had to be made like us in every way to become a merciful and faithful high priest. Shared humanity enables sympathy. He represents us before God because He is one of us.
Because He suffered when tempted, He can help those being tempted. Jesus knows temptation experientially, not theoretically. His help comes from solidarity, not distance.
